sentences.text: Yes, I worked in a company who was the [FRENCH].
They really were a steel, steel manufacturers and also anything that goes with steel, you know nails, screws, everything like that.
And why I mention it is because in those days, when there was no food, it was really nearly a starvation diet, we couldn't afford the black market.
So what we had to do is go into the countryside and try and find food in, in the villages in-- with the peasants.
And I must say, they were horrible to us because they were not so much interested in money.
They were interested in goods.
And where should we get goods from?
So the, the company was good enough to let us have-- which was difficult to obtain, except because we worked there-- nails or screws or anything like that that you could barter with them.
So we took it in turn.
Sometimes it was my turn.
Sometimes it was my father's turn, sometimes my big brother's turn to go to the country and find food.
How did you get there?
So we to-- we had to take a train from Saint-Etienne.
And although my father was dead against that I should have a bicycle-- but I did get a bicycle because I needed it to go to work.
And I put a bicycle on the train.
And from Le Puy, which is in the Haute-Loire, I used to take with my bike-- climb the mountains with a bike-- with a little-- I had a case and a rucksack.
And I used to leave in the evening.
And it wasn't heated.
It was freezing cold.
And I used to sleep in a little hotel where it was not heated.
And I was not covered properly.
And I was freezing and waiting for 4 o' clock in the morning to get up to get that-- the bus that took me a little bit farther.
And from there I took my bike.
And when I knocked at the farmer's house, I-- they spoke-- they didn't speak French.
They spoke a patois.
And that was very-- you couldn't understand it even if you knew French.
It was a language I didn't understand.
So you wanted to barter for a few potatoes, some flour, perhaps a bit of butter.
And soap sometimes-- you know, soap was very scarce.
So you had to beg them to let you have with, with-- for your nails, or screws, or whatever.
You had already several kilos of nails on you-- with you, you know, which was difficult on your bike, too.
And they were not the modern bikes of today.
You had to use your legs.
And I used to manage to get lentils and potatoes and flour and, and-- enough, you know, to fill up my, my rucksack and my, my case.
But when I came down the mountains and came into La Puy, I had to face the German Army there and the French police.
And you were not allowed to go out of the country and get food.
So I used to hide my bicycle behind the bench.
And I used to hide the food behind another bench.
And my heart just dropped into my shoes, you know, because I was trembling away-- I wasn't only 17-- and wait for the train to arrive.
And when the train arrived, you know, I quickly took all this.
And I had to manage the bicycle, the food, and everything to get into the train.
And when I arrived inside Saint-Etienne, the Germans were there.
And the French police was there.
And they were asking to open your luggage.
So every time it was a terrible experience to do that, to go up in the mountains, for everybody who had tried it.
I managed to get through every time.
My mother was standing on the other side.
And she was trembling for me to get through.
But it was the only way to survive.
How did you avoid the police and the-- and the Germans?
I don't know how I avoided it.
They were-- they let-- they let me pass.
There was a man behind-- in front of me who said-- they're asking, what have you got in your case?
And he said, I have a pig.
And they were laughing.
They thought he was just joking.
But, in fact, he had a pig in his-- [LAUGHS] in this case.
But that was the time that you went through so much anxiety and fear already.
Because you knew-- not only if he arrested you for the food, he arrested you as a Jew, too.
And you knew what it meant.
So these were the times.
To get food in was very hard.
Also, we, we heard-- while we lived there, from 1940 to '44-- although I went to work, you know, and I even did a lot of sports, you know, in the evening.
And so this probably helped me to, to, to be strong, you know, when I had to go through the camps.
It-- we lived in fear.
But-- as being Jews with a French nationality, we were not being taken yet.
We were a little bit protected, as I said.
But we were-- we had friends who were foreign Jews and were hidden.
And we knew exactly what was happening, that they were being taken.
What we never knew exactly-- and I must admit it-- is that we knew that the Jews were being sent to camps, you know, we thought to working camps.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although the-- somehow the world knew that in '42, there were already concentration camps, I don't know if we didn't-- why we didn't know that there were concentration camps or death camps.
We did not know.
My family didn't know and their friends didn't know.
They lived in fear.
They knew that people who were sent away hadn't come back.
But they didn't know that there were death camps.
This we really were not in the know-how and ignorant about it.

We knew about Dachau-- all these camps that the Germans opened already for their own people and for-- and for-- in Germany, where they sent all the unwanted ones they'd already they sent to Dachau and the terrible things they do to their own people that were against regime.
We followed all of that.

But-- and we, we also heard about the terrible things that were happening in, in occupied France, you know, because people smuggled through in, in the locomotives in the-- under the coals, you know, were hidden there with families, and came through to the demarcation line, you know, that that-- we heard all those horrible stories, that husbands were taken away and shot, that Resistance were taken and, and, and, and were caught.
Because my brother wanted to go in the Resistance.
And my mother wouldn't let him.
I don't know if she was right or not.
But she couldn't cope with it.

And after the war, we heard that some of his school friends who had joined the Maquis-- because we were very near the Maquis-- were unfortunately caught and, and, and, and tortured and killed that way.
They are horrible stories.
But here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we somehow-- we managed-- with all the fear we had and all the problems we had, we tried to survive, you understand?
We managed to get the food.
We managed to get some food that-- we were always hungry.
I mean, we were young people.
We were always hungry because the bread ration was very, very small-- I think one pound of bread for six people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the rest, the fat-- there was no fat.
There was no butter.
There was nothing.
People that were the black-- could buy the black market, they probably had.
And, of course, there were all the people who collaborated.
Am I speaking about '40 or '42 because now the whole of France is occupied?
This-- when we arrived there, although there was no food till '42, we were-- we only knew what was happening in the occupied France.
But it mustn't forget that in November '42, the whole of France was occupied.
Then that was a different story.
We still didn't wear a Star of David, which means that you were not necessarily recognized.
And I think, as we came from Lorraine, the people in the building didn't even know we were Jews.
They knew that we were Lorrains.
You know, they came from Alsace-Lorraine.
They were refugees.
That's about all.
I don't think they knew we were Jewish.
We frequented Jewish people.
But, you see, you had to be very careful to go to other people's houses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother had friends.
They were Hungarian-Jewish people.
And she went there from time to time to visit these people.
And then, when it became a little bit dangerous, she wouldn't go anymore.
But the thing was that she had a coal merchant in the same building.
And we had a ration of ration cards.
And our ration cards, we got plenty of wine.
And we are not-- we are not big wine drinkers.
So my mother bartered her wine for coals.
And this was only June.
But she wanted some to make a reserve in case we wouldn't be able to go out-- that we should have coals in the house.
And we wouldn't be-- we could be hidden and not go out.
It is by being in that house and coming out of that house from the coal merchant that the Milice, you know, in civilian clothes-- they-- wasn't it-- the Gestapo employed Milice.
Or, you know, the Vichy government employed Milice-- that they stopped my mother.
They were waiting.
They were-- they were going to arrest this Hungarian family.
But my mother found herself in that building.
So when they're asking for her identity card-- of course, it was a ration card-- there was 'Jew' on it.
Of course, and she had on her six cards.
And that's how they-- that's how they eventually caught us, all of us.
And they arrested my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they arrested the Hungarian lady and her son.
But the father wasn't home yet.
But I was coming home from work, and he saw me.
He waited for me in the street.
He said, don't go home, he said.
Your mother has been arrested.
But I had two younger brothers at home.
My father was out in the country to find food.
My oldest brother was in hospital for an operation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I rushed home.
I said, I have to go and see what the others are doing.
So I came home.
[CRYING] And I opened the door up.
And the-- and Armand, who was 17, and the little one who was 13, they said, a man was here from insurance company.
And he asked, where is your mother and father?
And I said, you were not home.
But as soon was I inside the door that there was a knock at the door.
And I quickly understood that it must be from the Milice.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I-- I said in patois Lorrain, I said to the children, the boys, to run away because the house we lived in was the-- was a ground floor from the backyard-- from the back windows, but the first floor from the front.
So I told them to run away.
While-- in the meantime, I was asking them for their papers.
And for-- to show their identity and what right they had to arrest us, to gain time.
So, of course, they showed me their papers.
And they rushed in, and they ran through the flat.
And they saw-- but they, they-- Armand wouldn't move.
He wanted to see what was happening to me.
The little one ran out of the window.
So first they looked into the flat.
Then they could see that they-- that he wasn't here, the younger one.
So they looked out of the window.
And, of course, Marcel was there.
He wanted to see what was happening to us.
And they brought him back.
I didn't get over that for years and years and years because he could have been saved.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he wanted to know what was happening to us.
Not only responsible, I felt that I gave him a chance.
And he didn't take it.
And he didn't take it.
And I didn't know what the future was holding for us.
So, of course, they arrested us.
That was-- that was May the-- May the 17th, 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they took us through a, a, a-- it's-- a caserne is a barrack, an army barrack, where they arrested the Jews, that they took them there.
There were raffles going on now all the time.
Now, by, by June-- by May, 1944 no Jew was spared.
Whoever they get-- they could have got hold of, they would arrest.
Because you mustn't forget that they were getting money for each Jew that they arrested.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we were taken first to the Gestapo and put into prison.
This is your mother, your--
No, my mother was already--
--in the barracks, away with the other people.
We were arrested in the evening.
So I was-- we were interrogated-- I was interrogated in the Gestapo.
And they asked questions.
The chief of the Gestapo was sitting there.
On the other side was a, a guard, a German guard with a dog.
And the Milice was outside.
He only asked questions.
And the guard, you know, from time to time he came over to get-- clobber you because he didn't like the answer or whatever.
And we were thrown again into the prison that was under the Gestapo headquarters.
And there, I kept saying to my young brother, but why didn't you run away?
Why didn't you run away?
Why didn't you run away?
The next day, they took us out, up again to the Gestapo, the, the Gestapo chief.
And that guy kept asking questions again.
They asked me, how many of you are you?
So I said, we are three.
Because he had arrested three of us.
Although the Gestapo saw-- could see that I wasn't telling the whole truth because they had the six cards, he didn't say anything.
But the Milice was listening to the conversation outside.
So he came in.
And he said, she's lying.
We arrested her brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What they had done--
Yes.
They had gone to the hospital.
And my brother was coming out of an eye operation, bandaged completely, in between two nuns.
And they come in-- and as-- you know, the Milice comes in civilian clothes.
They said, have you got David Silberberg?
They said, yes.
That's him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they took him, all with his eyes bandaged, to the prison-- to the-- [CRYING] to [FRENCH].

The next-- The thing was that these nuns-- when we came back he needed another operation, tonsil operation-- they said, we prayed all year for you because we didn't realize that was the Gestapo-- that was the, the Milice.
The Milice were the--
The Vichy government had formed a new-- a, a new kind of army, which was the Milice, which was a kind of SS, you know, it was a German SS.
They worked for the German government under Vichy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next day my father who was out looking for food, he came back.
And they were waiting for him.
So, you see, they got one after the other.
And they, they-- and eventually took us.
And then we were all in the barracks there.
From the 17th, we were there-- I write the date here-- till the 26th of May.
You know, it was-- the 17th of May was a beautiful day.
I had made arrangements for some friends to go to the countryside with our bicycles.
And it was Ascension Day in France.
And we were all looking forward to get away from Saint-Etienne.
And that was the day we were all arrested.
Yes, the sad part was, as it was getting so dangerous, my mother had found the week before in a convent in Le Puy, they were going to, to take us in.
You see, it was a matter of days.
We were now frightened.
There was only one thing to do-- is to leave Saint-Etienne.
So we were leaving in a few days' time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the cellar, we had a reserve of food, you know, eggs, and potatoes, and everything, in case we couldn't go out anymore.
You see, the situation was now bad.
And we were ready to go.
The sad part, the unfortunate part was that my mother was that day with the wine merchant.
And they were looking for the Hungarian family.
It was so unlucky and so tragic.
And my poor mother, you know, she couldn't forgive herself [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that she happened to be there at that very moment.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] By then-- by now we were all together in that-- in that-- in that barracks, army barracks.
And on the 26th, when they took us, we were now a whole train of Jewish people-- families.
They took us to the station.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Gestapo [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] chief came over to me.
And he said to me, if it wasn't for your Milice, you wouldn't be on that train.
You know, he was trying to clear his conscience.
Then he said, don't try to escape, he says.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, otherwise, the other members of your family will be shot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And the next thing he said, you know, we found amongst the things we took from your apartment, we found a typewriter.
I can-- we can do with it.
But can-- how much do you want for it?
An unbelievable story.
Have you got an address where I can find-- send the money?
We were-- he knew where we were going to be sent.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I gave the name of the company I worked for.
And hold and behold, when we came back after the war-- after the-- after the camps, this company said, you know, we have some money for you.
We don't know where it comes from.
Whatever it was, you know, 30 pounds, 40 pounds, and something.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's how, you know-- that's-- that was his-- there, he was going to evade-- if I try to run away, I would be shot.
On the other hand, I give you money for your typewriter.
How to explain that, I can't.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we arrived in Drancy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The most unbelievable sight-- because it was like a town itself, you know, because it was a kind of a little-- cheap housing estate, somehow.
And-- because when we leave Saint-Etienne, we are still in a proper train.
We are not in wagons.
And we arrived in Drancy.
We meet up with some very good friends of ours, Robert Weil and, and Fanny, a friend of mine from school, and two little children.
I said, how come you are here?
He said, yes, I saved 300 children.
He was member of the OSE.
[CRYING] And he was at the Swiss border.
And he was caught with his wife, who was 24, 23, and two little children-- four years and 18 months, something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were on their way to Switzerland because they went with the-- with the whole of the town to Cognac, to the other side.
And they were caught there, too, you see.
And they were now with us in the same transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were hundreds and hundreds of people there, thousands of them there.
And the conditions were terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we didn't know what was going to happen to us yet, you know.
And when-- we stayed-- a few days we stayed there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed there in Drancy three, three nights.
We leave on the 30th.
So we must have stayed from May till the-- from the 18th-- from the-- five days about, yes.
We stayed about five days in Drancy.
And then they took us to the-- a whole-- 2,000 people they took to the station.
There was one case where the lorries that were standing there, the person that was there-- there was a woman there with a girl of about 10 or 12.
And she pretended to be mad because she didn't want to go on a-- on, on, on the-- on the-- truck.
And I can see only by hindsight, but she knew what was happening better than we did.
So they took us to the station.
And there they put us in these trucks, you know.
And it was June, you mustn't forget.
A hundred, a hundred, a hundred people in a trucks, you know, more.
There was no room to-- there was no room to, to, to, to, to stretch your legs or do anything.
And there were no-- the conditions-- there were babies.
There were old people.

And there, in that-- in that convoy-- and they were already with us at the time in the-- in the-- in the barracks-- but we didn't know them-- was a family Dreyfus, a mother, father, and two girls, who lived in-- near Strasbourg before the war and were arrested in [FRENCH], not in St-Etienne-- outside.
French Jews for the last 300, 400 years-- they were with us.
It is the, the girls-- the-- one-- only one survived.
This is the girl who, after the war, married my brother.
She was all the time with me after that.
So we were, like, together in the same, same wagon in the same conditions.
It was hot.
It was smelly.
It was no water, no food, no nothing-- a few belongings, I don't know what.
And my mother only felt sorry for her children that they should be here in, in that wagon in these conditions and god knows what.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even my dress was torn from the-- from being so squashed together and hot and sweaty.
And you-- and, and no room to move.
So when I got up, somehow, my dress was torn, you know, from sitting like that.
When we arrived after three days in Birkenau, we must have arrived-- it was early morning, dawn, 5:00 or 6:00.
It was-- they opened those doors-- all what we had with air, a little air for a change.
But there were these-- at the time, we didn't know they were called 'Sonderkommandos,' these men in stripes.
Schnell, schnell, schnell!
And go-- you know, and so on and so forth.
We didn't know where we had arrived.
And they said, give the babies to the older women.
Older women-- my mother was 46.
So behind us was a young woman who had a baby.
And she gave it to my mother.
